one_line_takeaway: Reporting only a jailbreak attack's best-case ASR configuration is misleading: across PAIR and bijection learning on three open-source LLMs, tested-variant Union Coverage exceeds the best single-configuration ASR by 18-33 points, and the paper's proposed VSM metric shows the headline number can overstate the mean tested-space performance by up to ~5x (VSM 0.80-0.86 for bijection learning).
key_finding: PAIR: on Mistral-7B-Instruct, the best single template (Authority Endorsement, K=3) reaches 69% ASR, but the 3-template union at K=3 reaches 88% (union exceeds best template by 24/28/19 percentage points at K=1/2/3 respectively); full 9-configuration mean ASR is 39.7%, giving full-space VSM = 0.425. On Qwen3-0.6B, best single-template ASR is 75% (Authority Endorsement, K=3), union reaches 93% (gaps of 31/27/18 points across K=1/2/3); mean ASR across 9 configs is 51.6%, VSM = 0.312. Bijection learning: best-ASR/mean-ASR/VSM/Union-Coverage are 52.0%/7.08%/0.864/85.0% (85/100) on Qwen3-0.6B; 81.0%/15.94%/0.803/100.0% (100/100) on Mistral-7B; 17.0%/2.69%/0.842/43.0% (43/100) on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. On Mistral-7B the full 36-configuration ASR grid (Table 3) shows the best cell is FS=26,TS=1 at 81% ASR, but at that same FS, ASR ranges 49-81% depending on TS, and TS=1 alone uncovers configurations (small FS) that succeed at 17-28% while TS>=2 nearly zeroes them out -- illustrating that some prompts are only vulnerable under specific parameter interactions. Across both attack families and three open-source models, the Union Coverage vs. Best-ASR gap ranges from 18 to 33 percentage points, and bijection VSM is consistently 0.80-0.86 across all three models -- i.e. headline ASR overstates typical/mean performance by roughly a factor of five in that setting.
core_contribution: This is a position paper arguing that the standard practice of reporting a single best-case Attack Success Rate (ASR) for parameterised jailbreak attacks (e.g., varying system prompt templates, conversation rounds, encoding dispersion, teaching-shot counts) is incomplete because it hides both (a) how representative that best number is of typical performance across the tested variant space, and (b) how much additional attack surface (prompt-level coverage) is reachable by trying other variants. The authors propose two new metrics: Variant Sensitivity Measure (VSM = (Best ASR - Mean ASR) / Best ASR), which quantifies how far the headline ASR deviates from the mean ASR over the tested variant space, and Union Coverage (UC), the fraction of prompts jailbroken by at least one tested configuration. They argue both should be reported alongside per-variant ASR as a new minimum reporting standard for parameterised jailbreak evaluation, and back the position with two original empirical case studies (PAIR and bijection learning) run by the authors themselves.
threat_model: Not an attack/defense threat model in the conventional sense -- this is an evaluation-methodology paper. The implicit 'threat' framing is: a defender who only sees a paper's headline best-case ASR may underestimate the reachable attack surface, since different parameter configurations of the same jailbreak family (e.g., different PAIR system-prompt templates, or different bijection dispersion/teaching-shot settings) succeed on different, only partially overlapping subsets of harmful prompts. The paper does not propose a new attack or defense; it characterizes measurement/reporting risk in the jailbreak-evaluation literature itself.
limitations: Empirical evidence is restricted to two attack families (PAIR, bijection learning) and three open-source target models (Mistral-7B-Instruct, Qwen3-0.6B, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct); no closed-source/frontier models evaluated due to API cost of exhaustive variant enumeration. The PAIR study fixes N=1 (single sampling stream) to isolate template/depth effects, explicitly not covering the full PAIR parameter space (parallel streams, attacker-model choice, stopping criteria). The authors acknowledge exhaustive enumeration is impractical for attacks with very large parameter spaces (e.g., PAIR, GCG) and that broader cross-attack validation (AutoDAN, GCG, TAP, CodeAttack) remains future work. As a position paper, the proposed VSM/UC metrics are new and not yet independently validated or adopted by the community; the paper argues for but does not enforce or benchmark community adoption.

datasets_used: HarmBench (100-prompt subset, stratified across the 7 harm categories in proportion to the full 400-prompt HarmBench set)
quantitative_results: PAIR Mistral-7B-Instruct: best single-template ASR (Authority Endorsement, K=3) = 69%; 3-template union ASR at K=3 = 88%; full 9-config mean ASR = 39.7%; VSM = 0.425
PAIR Qwen3-0.6B: best single-template ASR (Authority Endorsement, K=3) = 75%; 3-template union ASR at K=3 = 93%; full 9-config mean ASR = 51.6%; VSM = 0.312
Bijection learning Qwen3-0.6B: Best ASR = 52.0%, Mean ASR = 7.08%, Worst ASR = 0.0%, VSM = 0.864, Union Coverage = 85.0% (85/100)
Bijection learning Mistral-7B: Best ASR = 81.0%, Mean ASR = 15.94%, Worst ASR = 0.0%, VSM = 0.803, Union Coverage = 100.0% (100/100)
Bijection learning Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct: Best ASR = 17.0%, Mean ASR = 2.69%, Worst ASR = 0.0%, VSM = 0.842, Union Coverage = 43.0% (43/100)
Union Coverage exceeds Best-configuration ASR by 18 to 33 percentage points across the two attack families and three target models tested
Cited (Related Work, not this paper's result): CodeAttack (Ren et al. 2024) ASR on Claude-2 rises from 24% under Python encoding to 74% under Go encoding
baselines_compared: PAIR (Chao et al. 2025) -- 3 system-prompt templates (Roleplay, Logical Appeal, Authority Endorsement) x 3 conversation depths (K=1,2,3), attacker model Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct
Bijection learning (Huang et al. 2024) -- 6 dispersion levels (FS in {0,5,10,13,18,26}) x 6 teaching-shot counts (TS in {1,2,4,6,8,10}), 36 configurations per target model
